repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
<!--
  Thank you for contributing to Neovim!
  If this is your first time, check out https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#pull-requests-prs
  for our PR guidelines.
-->

## Problem

`vim.pack` doesn't provide a code action for updating all plugins.

## Solution

Offer a new code action (scoped to the source file) for updating all plugins.

comment_data.body: Although this isn't documented in the protocol, it matches VS Code's behavior: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/9b7643f90393b9ad2c5d5cbbdad70fa928090009/src/vs/editor/contrib/codeAction/common/types.ts#L71
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -195,36 +197,66 @@ end
 
 --- @alias vim.pack.lsp.CodeActionContext { diagnostics: table, only: table?, triggerKind: integer? }
 
+--- @param bufnr integer
+--- @return boolean
+local function has_pending_updates(bufnr)
+  local in_update = false
+  for _, l in ipairs(vim.api.nvim_buf_get_lines(bufnr, 0, -1, false)) do
+    local group = l:match(group_header_pattern)
+    if group then
+      in_update = group == 'Update'
+    elseif in_update and l:match(plugin_header_pattern) then
+      return true
+    end
+  end
+  return false
+end
+
 --- @param params { textDocument: { uri: string }, range: vim.pack.lsp.Range, context: vim.pack.lsp.CodeActionContext }
 --- @param callback function
 methods['textDocument/codeAction'] = function(params, callback)
   local bufnr = get_confirm_bufnr(params.textDocument.uri)
-  local empty_kind = vim.lsp.protocol.CodeActionKind.Empty
-  local only = params.context.only or { empty_kind }
-  if not (bufnr and vim.tbl_contains(only, empty_kind)) then
-    return callback(nil, {})
-  end
-  local plug_data = get_plug_data_at_lnum(bufnr, params.range.start.line + 1)
-  if not plug_data.name then
+  if not bufnr then
     return callback(nil, {})
   end
+  local empty_kind = vim.lsp.protocol.CodeActionKind.Empty
+  local source_kind = vim.lsp.protocol.CodeActionKind.Source
+  local only = params.context.only
 
-  local function new_action(title, command)
-    return {
-      title = ('%s `%s`'):format(title, plug_data.name),
-      command = { title = title, command = command, arguments = { bufnr, plug_data } },
-    }
+  local res = {}
+
+  -- Only advertise the source-level action when the client explicitly asks
+  -- for `source` actions via `only`.

comment_data.body: As far as I remember, the *very* strict rule about LSP related features in Nvim is "it must be part of the official specification". Any other functionality is out of scope and even not welcome. I've seen this applied several times very strictly.

comment_data.body: Indeed, I've often been the one enforcing such mentality. However this isn't a deviation from the protocol, I'm just matching the behavior with what I believe most users are familiar with (or expect). I'm also open for us to do something different than VS Code if we desire.

comment_data.body:
> However this isn't a deviation from the protocol, I'm just matching the behavior with what I believe most users are familiar with (or expect).

I would still consider important to uniformly follow the established rules about LSP related functionality. If it is strict, it must be strict everywhere.

comment_regular_data.body:
The core idea behind code actions is to provide actions for a "plugin at cursor". For acting on all plugins there are `:write` and `:quit`.

This adds duplicating functionality via non trivial code complexity, for which I am really hesitant. Is there any particular reason for having this?

> The core idea behind code actions is to provide actions for a "plugin at cursor". 

I disagree. This is exactly why the `CodeActionKind` of `source` exists (which is what I'm using here). Examples of useful refactors like that are "Add all missing imports" or "Fix all lint errors", which other popular LSP servers provide.

> For acting on all plugins there are `:write` and `:quit`.

Those aren't as discoverable as code actions. I like having a consistent flow via LSP actions that allows me to update all plugins via the same mechanism.

> This adds duplicating functionality via non trivial code complexity, for which I am really hesitant.

I don't consider duplication to be a valid counterargument here. If so then why did we add `:packupdate` when you can use `:=vim.pack.update()`?

> > The core idea behind code actions is to provide actions for a "plugin at cursor".
> 
> I disagree. This is exactly why the `CodeActionKind` of `source` exists (which is what I'm using here). Examples of useful refactors like that are "Add all missing imports" or "Fix all lint errors", which other popular LSP servers provide.

It was not about code actions in general, but about code actions for `vim.pack` confirmation buffer. As the "global actions" are handled via `:write` and `:quit`.

> Those aren't as discoverable as code actions. I like having a consistent flow via LSP actions that allows me to update all plugins via the same mechanism.

As interacting with users shows, it is in reverse: code actions are not as discoverable as `:write` for the buffer that is named "confirm".

> I don't consider duplication to be a valid counterargument here. If so then why did we add `:packupdate` when you can use `:=vim.pack.update()`?

The one is intended for interactive use and the other - for programmatic (that was suggested for interactive use for too long). Here both `:write` and "update all plugins" code action are interactive. I.e. two ways of doing the same thing.

---

I am still hesitant about adding this.

> I am still hesitant about adding this.

Feel free to close this then. I won't add an unwelcome feature.

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

title: feat(pack): code action for updating all plugins
